rawText:
Imagine flipping a coin ten times and getting heads seven out of ten — you might start to wonder whether the coin is unfair, even though a perfectly fair coin can easily produce that result by chance alone.

Now imagine flipping the same coin ten thousand times: if heads still comes up seventy percent of the time, you would have a much stronger reason to suspect bias, because the larger sample makes random variation far less likely to explain the pattern.

This simple example captures one of the most important principles in statistics: small samples are unreliable because they are heavily influenced by chance, and the patterns they reveal may not hold up once more data is collected.

In everyday life, people routinely draw conclusions from small samples without realizing it.

A traveler who visits a country for three days and meets a few rude shopkeepers might conclude that the entire nation is unfriendly, even though a larger and more representative experience would likely tell a very different story.

A manager who interviews four candidates and finds that the best one graduated from a particular university might start favoring applicants from that school, even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental.

Psychologists call this tendency the "law of small numbers" — the mistaken belief that a small sample should mirror the properties of the larger population it was drawn from.

In reality, small samples are noisy: they fluctuate wildly from one draw to the next, and the conclusions they suggest are unstable.

This principle has practical consequences far beyond academic research.

Product reviews based on five ratings are far less informative than those based on five hundred, yet consumers often treat them with equal seriousness.

Medical case reports describing a handful of patients can generate dramatic headlines, but without larger trials, there is no reliable way to separate a genuine treatment effect from random variation or placebo response.

Even experienced professionals fall prey to small-sample thinking — doctors, investors, and coaches all tend to overweight patterns they have personally observed, forgetting that their experience is itself a small and potentially unrepresentative sample.

The remedy is not to ignore small datasets entirely but to treat them as preliminary signals that require confirmation before they can guide important decisions.

Asking "how large is the sample?" is one of the simplest and most powerful questions a critical thinker can learn to ask, and it applies equally to scientific papers, business reports, and the stories we tell ourselves about the world.

sentences.text: If you flip a coin just a few times, the results can be tricky and make you think wrong things.
sentences.translation: 동전을 몇 번만 던지면, 결과가 헷갈려서 잘못된 생각을 하게 될 수 있어요.

sentences.text: When we see only a little bit of something, we might think it tells us the whole story, but it usually does not.
sentences.translation: 무언가의 작은 부분만 보면 전체를 알 수 있다고 생각할 수 있지만, 대부분 그렇지 않아요.

sentences.text: For example, meeting a few mean people in another country does not mean everyone there is mean.
sentences.translation: 예를 들어, 다른 나라에서 못된 사람 몇 명을 만났다고 거기 모든 사람이 못됐다는 뜻은 아니에요.

sentences.text: When only five people rate something online, that rating is not very helpful compared to hundreds of ratings.
sentences.translation: 온라인에서 다섯 명만 평가하면, 그 평점은 수백 개의 평가에 비해 별로 도움이 안 돼요.

sentences.text: Even smart adults like doctors and coaches can be fooled by small amounts of information.
sentences.translation: 의사나 코치 같은 똑똑한 어른들도 적은 양의 정보에 속을 수 있어요.

sentences.text: The best thing to do is always ask "how many examples are there?" before believing something.
sentences.translation: 무언가를 믿기 전에 "예시가 몇 개나 있어?"라고 항상 물어보는 것이 가장 좋아요.

sentences.text: The article explains why conclusions drawn from small samples are unreliable because chance heavily influences the results.
sentences.translation: 이 글은 우연이 결과에 크게 영향을 미치기 때문에 작은 표본에서 도출한 결론이 왜 신뢰할 수 없는지 설명해요.

sentences.text: People naturally tend to assume that a small number of observations reflects the whole population, which psychologists call the "law of small numbers."
sentences.translation: 사람들은 자연적으로 적은 수의 관찰이 전체 모집단을 반영한다고 가정하는데, 심리학자들은 이를 "소수의 법칙"이라 불러요.

sentences.text: Everyday examples include judging an entire country after a brief visit or favoring a university based on one impressive graduate.
sentences.translation: 일상적인 예로는 짧은 방문 후 나라 전체를 판단하거나, 한 명의 인상적인 졸업생을 근거로 대학교를 선호하는 것이 있어요.

sentences.text: This problem extends to product reviews, medical reports, and professional decision-making, where small data creates misleading patterns.
sentences.translation: 이 문제는 제품 리뷰, 의료 보고서, 전문적 의사결정으로 확장되는데, 적은 데이터가 오해를 부르는 패턴을 만들어요.

sentences.text: Even experts like doctors and investors fall prey to overweighting their own limited personal experience.
sentences.translation: 의사나 투자자 같은 전문가들도 자신의 제한된 개인 경험에 과도한 비중을 두는 함정에 빠져요.

sentences.text: The solution is to treat small datasets as preliminary signals and always ask how large the sample is before drawing conclusions.
sentences.translation: 해결책은 작은 데이터를 예비 신호로 취급하고, 결론을 내리기 전에 표본이 얼마나 큰지 항상 질문하는 거예요.

sentences.text: This expository essay uses the coin-flip metaphor to introduce the statistical principle that small samples are disproportionately influenced by random variation.
sentences.translation: 이 설명적 에세이는 동전 던지기 비유를 사용해 작은 표본이 무작위 변동에 의해 불균형적으로 영향을 받는다는 통계 원리를 도입해요.

sentences.text: The writer employs a deductive structure, moving from abstract statistical principle to concrete everyday examples to professional and scientific domains.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 추상적 통계 원리에서 구체적 일상 사례로, 다시 전문적·과학적 영역으로 이동하는 연역적 구조를 활용해요.

sentences.text: Technical vocabulary — "random variation," "placebo response," "representative sample" — is embedded in accessible prose, modulating difficulty for a general but educated audience.
sentences.translation: "무작위 변동", "위약 반응", "대표 표본" 같은 전문 용어가 접근 가능한 문체에 내재되어 있어, 교양 있는 일반 독자를 위해 난이도를 조절해요.

sentences.text: The essay draws on Tversky and Kahneman's "law of small numbers" framework without citation, assuming cultural literacy of the concept among informed readers.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 트버스키와 카너먼의 "소수의 법칙" 틀을 인용 없이 차용하며, 교양 있는 독자들의 개념적 문해력을 전제해요.

sentences.text: Hedging language — "may not hold up," "almost certainly coincidental," "in many cases" — maintains epistemic caution appropriate to a piece about the dangers of overconfident inference.
sentences.translation: "유지되지 않을 수 있다", "거의 확실히 우연적인", "많은 경우에" 같은 완화 표현이 과신된 추론의 위험에 관한 글에 적합한 인식론적 신중함을 유지해요.

sentences.text: The concluding sentence reframes the entire argument as a single actionable question — "how large is the sample?" — converting abstract statistical literacy into a practical critical-thinking tool.
sentences.translation: 결론 문장은 전체 논증을 하나의 실행 가능한 질문 — "표본이 얼마나 큰가?" — 으로 재구성하며, 추상적 통계 문해력을 실용적 비판적 사고 도구로 전환해요.

sentences.original: Imagine flipping a coin ten times and getting heads seven out of ten — you might start to wonder whether the coin is unfair, even though a perfectly fair coin can easily produce that result by chance alone.
sentences.translation: 동전을 열 번 던져서 열 번 중 일곱 번 앞면이 나오는 것을 상상해 보라 — 완벽하게 공정한 동전도 우연만으로 그 결과를 쉽게 만들어낼 수 있음에도, 당신은 동전이 불공정한지 의심하기 시작할 수 있다.

grammarElements.text: flipping a coin ten times and getting heads seven out of ten
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (동명사구 2개 병렬)

grammarElements.text: you might start to wonder whether the coin is unfair
grammarElements.label: 대시 뒤 주절 (조동사 might + whether절)

grammarElements.text: even though a perfectly fair coin can easily produce that result by chance alone
grammarElements.label: 양보절 (even though)

vocabulary.example: Flipping a coin is a common way to make a random decision.
An unfair coin lands on heads more often than tails.
The result could have occurred by chance alone.

expressions.usage: Imagine + 동명사 구조로 독자의 참여를 유도하는 도입 표현
비율이나 빈도를 구체적으로 제시할 때 쓰는 표현

patterns.pattern: Imagine [gerund A] and [gerund B] — you might [reaction], even though [rational explanation]
patterns.explanation: 독자가 직접 상황을 상상하도록 유도한 뒤, 대시로 직관적 반응을 보여주고 even though로 합리적 설명을 대비시키는 도입 패턴.
examples: Imagine eating at a restaurant twice and getting sick both times — you might start to avoid the place, even though the food quality may have been fine.
Imagine meeting three unfriendly neighbors — you might conclude the whole building is hostile, even though most residents are perfectly pleasant.

drillQuestions.question: Imagine _____ a coin ten times.
You might start to wonder whether the coin is _____.
A perfectly fair coin can produce that result by _____ alone.
"Even though" introduces a _____ to the main idea.

sentences.notes: 글의 도입부로, Imagine + 동명사 구조로 독자를 즉시 사고 실험에 참여시킨다. 대시(—) 뒤에 직관적 반응(you might wonder)을 제시한 후, even though로 통계적 현실을 대비시킨다. 인지적 착각과 확률의 긴장을 설정하는 문장이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Imagine flipping a coin ten times
and getting heads seven out of ten
koreanAlignment.ko: 열 번 중 일곱 번 앞면이 나오는 것을 상상해 보라

koreanAlignment.en: you might start to wonder whether the coin is unfair
koreanAlignment.ko: 동전이 불공정한지 의심하기 시작할 수 있다

koreanAlignment.en: even though a perfectly fair coin can easily produce that result
koreanAlignment.ko: 완벽하게 공정한 동전도 그 결과를 쉽게 만들어낼 수 있음에도

koreanAlignment.en: by chance alone

pronunciationNotes: imagine: /ɪˈmædʒɪn/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "이매진"에 가깝다. g는 /dʒ/로 발음.
whether: /ˈweðər/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "웨더"에 가깝다. weather와 동음이의어.

sentences.original: Now imagine flipping the same coin ten thousand times: if heads still comes up seventy percent of the time, you would have a much stronger reason to suspect bias, because the larger sample makes random variation far less likely to explain the pattern.
sentences.translation: 이제 같은 동전을 만 번 던지는 것을 상상해 보라: 만약 앞면이 여전히 70퍼센트의 확률로 나온다면, 더 큰 표본이 무작위 변동으로 패턴을 설명할 가능성을 훨씬 낮추기 때문에, 편향을 의심할 훨씬 더 강한 이유가 생길 것이다.

grammarElements.text: Now imagine flipping the same coin ten thousand times
grammarElements.label: 명령형 (시간 전환 부사 Now)

grammarElements.text: if heads still comes up seventy percent of the time
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

grammarElements.text: you would have a much stronger reason to suspect bias
grammarElements.label: 주절 (가정법 would)

grammarElements.text: because the larger sample makes random variation far less likely to explain the pattern
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (사역동사 make + 형용사 보어)

vocabulary.example: A larger sample produces more reliable results.
Random variation can produce misleading patterns in small datasets.
The larger sample gives stronger evidence of bias.

expressions.usage: Now로 시간적·논리적 전환을 표시하며 사고 실험을 확장하는 표현
far가 비교급 less를 강조하여 확률의 극적 감소를 나타낸다

patterns.pattern: Now imagine [same scenario at larger scale]: if [same result], you would have a much stronger reason to [conclusion], because [statistical explanation]
patterns.explanation: 첫 문장의 사고 실험을 규모만 확대하여 반복한 뒤, 결론이 어떻게 달라지는지를 보여주는 대비 패턴. 표본 크기의 효과를 직관적으로 전달한다.
examples: Now imagine surveying ten thousand people instead of ten: if the same trend appears, you would have a much stronger reason to believe it is real, because the larger sample reduces the influence of outliers.
Now imagine testing the drug on five thousand patients: if the benefit persists, you would have a much stronger reason to trust the result, because random noise is minimized.

drillQuestions.question: The larger _____ makes random variation far less likely to explain the pattern.
You would have a much _____ reason to suspect bias.
"Random variation" means differences caused by _____.
"Far less likely" means the probability is much _____.

sentences.notes: "Now imagine"으로 시간적 전환과 규모 확대를 동시에 표시한다. 콜론 뒤에 가정법(if ~ would)이 이어져 첫 문장과의 대비를 완성한다. "makes random variation far less likely"에서 사역동사 make + 형용사 보어 구조가 핵심이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Now imagine flipping the same coin ten thousand times
koreanAlignment.ko: 이제 같은 동전을 만 번 던지는 것을 상상해 보라

koreanAlignment.en: if heads still comes up seventy percent of the time
you would have a much stronger reason to suspect bias
koreanAlignment.ko: 편향을 의심할 훨씬 더 강한 이유가 생길 것이다

koreanAlignment.en: because the larger sample makes random variation far less likely
koreanAlignment.ko: 더 큰 표본이 무작위 변동의 가능성을 훨씬 낮추기 때문에

koreanAlignment.en: to explain the pattern

pronunciationNotes: variation: /ˌveəriˈeɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "베어리에이션"에 가깝다. vary에서 파생.
bias: /ˈbaɪəs/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "바이어스"에 가깝다. 두 음절로 발음.

sentences.original: This simple example captures one of the most important principles in statistics: small samples are unreliable because they are heavily influenced by chance, and the patterns they reveal may not hold up once more data is collected.
sentences.translation: 이 간단한 예시는 통계학에서 가장 중요한 원리 중 하나를 포착한다: 작은 표본은 우연에 의해 크게 좌우되기 때문에 신뢰할 수 없으며, 그것들이 드러내는 패턴은 더 많은 데이터가 수집되면 유지되지 않을 수 있다.

grammarElements.text: one of the most important principles in statistics
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (최상급 포함 명사구)

grammarElements.text: small samples are unreliable because they are heavily influenced by chance
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절 (because 이유)

grammarElements.text: and the patterns they reveal may not hold up once more data is collected
grammarElements.label: 병렬절 (조동사 may + 시간부사절 once)

vocabulary.example: This example captures the core idea perfectly.
Small samples produce unreliable conclusions.
The theory did not hold up under further testing.

expressions.phrase: one of the most important principles
expressions.usage: 핵심 원리를 강조하며 도입할 때 쓰는 격식적 표현
may로 불확실성을, hold up으로 지속 가능성을 나타내는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: This [adjective] example captures one of the most important principles in [field]: [principle statement], and [consequence with may]
patterns.explanation: 앞선 사고 실험을 요약하며 핵심 원리를 콜론 뒤에 명시하고, and절로 실질적 함의를 덧붙이는 논증 전환 패턴.
examples: This everyday scenario captures one of the most important principles in psychology: first impressions are disproportionately powerful, and the judgments they produce may not reflect reality.
This basic experiment captures one of the most important principles in medicine: correlation does not prove causation, and the patterns observed may disappear in controlled trials.

drillQuestions.question: Small samples are _____ because they are heavily influenced by chance.
The patterns they reveal may not _____ once more data is collected.
"Heavily influenced" means influenced to a _____ degree.
This sentence introduces a key principle in _____.

sentences.notes: 사고 실험에서 원리 진술로의 전환을 수행하는 핵심 문장. 콜론이 원리를 도입하고, "may not hold up"이라는 완화 표현이 학술적 신중함을 유지한다. "once more data is collected"의 시간부사절이 조건을 명시한다.

koreanAlignment.en: This simple example captures
one of the most important principles in statistics
koreanAlignment.ko: 통계학에서 가장 중요한 원리 중 하나를

koreanAlignment.en: small samples are unreliable
because they are heavily influenced by chance
and the patterns they reveal may not hold up once more data is collected
koreanAlignment.ko: 그것들이 드러내는 패턴은 더 많은 데이터가 수집되면 유지되지 않을 수 있다

pronunciationNotes: statistics: /stəˈtɪstɪks/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "스터티스틱스"에 가깝다. 첫 s와 t가 빠르게 이어진다.
unreliable: /ˌʌnrɪˈlaɪəbəl/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "언릴라이어블"에 가깝다. un- 접두사가 부정을 나타낸다.

sentences.original: In everyday life, people routinely draw conclusions from small samples without realizing it.
sentences.translation: 일상생활에서 사람들은 그것을 인식하지 못한 채 작은 표본에서 일상적으로 결론을 끌어낸다.

vocabulary.example: People routinely underestimate how long tasks will take.
You should not draw conclusions from limited evidence.
We often make mistakes without realizing it.

expressions.usage: 증거나 데이터로부터 판단을 내리는 행위를 가리키는 학술적 표현
무의식적 행동을 나타내는 부사구

patterns.pattern: In [context], people routinely [verb] from [source] without realizing it
patterns.explanation: 배경을 설정한 뒤, 사람들이 무의식적으로 범하는 인지적 오류를 간결하게 진술하는 전환 패턴.
examples: In professional settings, people routinely form opinions from first impressions without realizing it.
In daily conversations, people routinely generalize from personal anecdotes without realizing it.

drillQuestions.question: People routinely draw _____ from small samples.
"Without realizing it" means people do this _____.
"Routinely" means something happens _____.
This sentence shifts the discussion from statistics to _____ life.

sentences.notes: 통계 원리에서 일상 적용으로의 전환을 수행하는 짧은 교량 문장. "routinely"와 "without realizing it"이 이 오류의 보편성과 무의식성을 강조한다. 간결함이 오히려 메시지의 충격을 높인다.

koreanAlignment.en: In everyday life
people routinely draw conclusions
from small samples
without realizing it

pronunciationNotes: routinely: /ruːˈtiːnli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "루틴리"에 가깝다. routine에 -ly 접미사.
conclusions: /kənˈkluːʒənz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "컨클루전즈"에 가깝다. s는 /ʒ/로 발음.

sentences.original: A traveler who visits a country for three days and meets a few rude shopkeepers might conclude that the entire nation is unfriendly, even though a larger and more representative experience would likely tell a very different story.
sentences.translation: 3일간 한 나라를 방문하고 무례한 상점 주인 몇 명을 만난 여행자는 나라 전체가 불친절하다고 결론지을 수 있는데, 더 크고 대표적인 경험은 아마 매우 다른 이야기를 해 줄 것임에도 그렇다.

grammarElements.text: A traveler who visits a country for three days and meets a few rude shopkeepers
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계절 포함 긴 명사구)

grammarElements.text: that the entire nation is unfriendly
grammarElements.label: that 명사절 (목적어)

grammarElements.text: even though a larger and more representative experience would likely tell a very different story
grammarElements.label: 양보절 (even though + 가정법 would)

vocabulary.example: A representative sample reflects the whole population.
He concluded that the city was dangerous after one bad experience.
You cannot judge an entire country from a brief visit.

expressions.usage: might로 가능성을, conclude that으로 판단의 내용을 제시하는 학술적 표현
실제로는 다른 결론이 나올 수 있다는 것을 비유적으로 나타내는 표현

patterns.pattern: A [person] who [limited experience] might conclude that [overgeneralization], even though [larger experience] would likely [different conclusion]
patterns.explanation: 제한된 경험에서 성급한 일반화를 이끌어내는 사례를 제시한 뒤, even though로 더 넓은 경험이 다른 결론을 줄 수 있음을 대비시키는 패턴.
examples: A student who fails two exams might conclude that the subject is impossible, even though more practice would likely reveal manageable patterns.
A diner who eats at one bad restaurant might conclude that the neighborhood has terrible food, even though a broader search would likely uncover excellent options.

drillQuestions.question: A traveler who meets rude shopkeepers might conclude the nation is _____.
A more _____ experience would tell a different story.
"Might conclude" indicates that the conclusion is _____.
"Tell a very different story" means give a very different _____.

sentences.notes: 소표본 오류의 첫 번째 구체적 사례. 관계절(who visits ~ and meets ~)이 긴 주어를 형성하고, "the entire nation"이라는 과잉 일반화와 "a larger and more representative experience"라는 교정을 대비시킨다. "representative"는 통계적 대표성 개념을 일상어로 전달하는 핵심 어휘이다.

koreanAlignment.en: A traveler who visits a country for three days
and meets a few rude shopkeepers
koreanAlignment.ko: 무례한 상점 주인 몇 명을 만난 여행자는

koreanAlignment.en: might conclude that the entire nation is unfriendly
koreanAlignment.ko: 나라 전체가 불친절하다고 결론지을 수 있는데

koreanAlignment.en: even though a larger and more representative experience
would likely tell a very different story
koreanAlignment.ko: 아마 매우 다른 이야기를 해 줄 것임에도 그렇다

pronunciationNotes: representative: /ˌreprɪˈzentətɪv/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "레프리젠터티브"에 가깝다. 다섯 음절.
conclude: /kənˈkluːd/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "컨클루드"에 가깝다. conclusion과 같은 어근.

sentences.original: A manager who interviews four candidates and finds that the best one graduated from a particular university might start favoring applicants from that school, even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental.
sentences.translation: 네 명의 지원자를 면접하고 가장 우수한 사람이 특정 대학 출신이라는 것을 발견한 관리자는 그 학교 출신 지원자를 편애하기 시작할 수 있는데, 그 연결 관계는 거의 확실히 우연에 불과함에도 그렇다.

grammarElements.text: A manager who interviews four candidates and finds that the best one graduated from a particular university
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계절 + that절 내포)

grammarElements.text: even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental
grammarElements.label: 양보절 (even though + 부사 강조)

vocabulary.example: The manager started favoring candidates from elite schools.
The company received hundreds of applicants.
The similarity between the two cases was purely coincidental.

expressions.usage: might start + 동명사로 점진적 행동 변화의 가능성을 나타낸다
almost certainly로 높은 확신을, coincidental로 우연성을 표현하는 학술적 완화

patterns.pattern: A [professional] who [observes small pattern] might start [biased behavior], even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental
patterns.explanation: 전문적 맥락에서 소표본 패턴이 편향된 행동으로 이어지는 과정을 보여주는 두 번째 사례 패턴. "almost certainly coincidental"이 통계적 판단을 내포한다.
examples: A teacher who notices that two quiet students scored highest might start favoring introverted learners, even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental.
An investor who profits twice from tech stocks might start ignoring other sectors, even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental.

drillQuestions.question: The manager might start _____ applicants from that school.
The connection between the university and performance is almost certainly _____.
"Applicants" are people who _____ for a position.
This example shows bias caused by a _____ sample.

sentences.notes: 두 번째 구체적 사례로, 직장 환경에서의 소표본 편향을 보여준다. 첫 사례(여행자)와 동일한 구문 패턴([A person] who ~ might ~ even though ~)을 반복하여 논증의 일관성을 강화한다. "almost certainly coincidental"이 통계적 사고를 일상적 맥락에 연결한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A manager who interviews four candidates
and finds that the best one graduated from a particular university
koreanAlignment.ko: 가장 우수한 사람이 특정 대학 출신이라는 것을 발견한 관리자는

koreanAlignment.en: might start favoring applicants from that school
koreanAlignment.ko: 그 학교 출신 지원자를 편애하기 시작할 수 있는데

koreanAlignment.en: even though the connection is almost certainly coincidental
koreanAlignment.ko: 그 연결 관계는 거의 확실히 우연에 불과함에도 그렇다

pronunciationNotes: coincidental: /koʊˌɪnsɪˈdentəl/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "코인시덴틀"에 가깝다. coincidence에서 파생.
applicants: /ˈæplɪkənts/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "애플리컨츠"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Psychologists call this tendency the "law of small numbers" — the mistaken belief that a small sample should mirror the properties of the larger population it was drawn from.
sentences.translation: 심리학자들은 이 경향을 "소수의 법칙"이라 부른다 — 작은 표본이 그것이 추출된 더 큰 모집단의 특성을 반영해야 한다는 잘못된 믿음이다.

grammarElements.text: the mistaken belief that a small sample should mirror the properties of the larger population it was drawn from
grammarElements.label: 대시 뒤 동격구 (that 동격절 + 관계절)

vocabulary.example: Humans have a tendency to see patterns where none exist.
A small sample does not reliably mirror the population.
The sample must be large enough to represent the population.
The data was drawn from a national survey.

expressions.usage: 학술 용어를 도입할 때 쓰는 전형적인 명명 구문
오류를 정의할 때 쓰는 격식적 동격 표현

patterns.pattern: [Experts] call this tendency the "[term]" — the mistaken belief that [incorrect assumption]
patterns.explanation: 학술 용어를 명명한 뒤, 대시로 동격구를 붙여 그 의미를 풀어 설명하는 정의 패턴. 5형식 동사(call A B)가 핵심이다.
examples: Economists call this tendency the "sunk cost fallacy" — the mistaken belief that money already spent should influence future decisions.
Sociologists call this tendency the "halo effect" — the mistaken belief that one positive trait guarantees overall excellence.

drillQuestions.question: Psychologists call this tendency the "law of _____ numbers."
The "law of small numbers" is a _____ belief.
A sample should not necessarily _____ the population.
"Drawn from" means _____ from a larger group.

sentences.notes: 핵심 학술 용어("law of small numbers")를 도입하는 정의 문장. 5형식(call A B)으로 명명하고, 대시 뒤에 동격구로 의미를 풀어낸다. "it was drawn from"의 관계절이 통계적 추출 개념을 자연스럽게 내포한다. Tversky와 Kahneman의 개념을 인용 없이 활용한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Psychologists call this tendency
the "law of small numbers"
the mistaken belief that
a small sample should mirror the properties
of the larger population it was drawn from

pronunciationNotes: psychologists: /saɪˈkɑːlədʒɪsts/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "사이칼러지스츠"에 가깝다. p는 묵음.
population: /ˌpɑːpjəˈleɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "파퓰레이션"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: In reality, small samples are noisy: they fluctuate wildly from one draw to the next, and the conclusions they suggest are unstable.
sentences.translation: 실제로 작은 표본은 잡음이 많다: 한 번 추출할 때마다 크게 변동하며, 그것들이 시사하는 결론은 불안정하다.

grammarElements.text: they fluctuate wildly from one draw to the next
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절 1

grammarElements.text: and the conclusions they suggest are unstable
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절 2 (병렬)

vocabulary.example: Noisy data makes it hard to identify true patterns.
Small-sample results fluctuate wildly between trials.
Conclusions from tiny samples are inherently unstable.

expressions.usage: 앞서 언급한 잘못된 믿음과 실제 현실을 대비할 때 쓰는 전환 표현

expressions.phrase: from one draw to the next
expressions.usage: 반복되는 과정에서의 변동을 나타내는 표현. draw는 통계적 추출을 의미

patterns.pattern: In reality, [subject] are [negative adjective]: they [verb] wildly, and the [noun] they [produce] are [negative adjective]
patterns.explanation: "In reality"로 오해를 교정한 뒤, 콜론으로 구체적 특성을 병렬로 설명하는 패턴. 두 개의 부정적 형용사(noisy, unstable)가 대조의 핵심이다.
examples: In reality, early prototypes are fragile: they break easily under stress, and the results they produce are inconsistent.
In reality, first drafts are rough: they shift direction unpredictably, and the arguments they contain are incomplete.

drillQuestions.question: In statistics, "noisy" data means data with a lot of _____.
Small samples _____ wildly from one draw to the next.
The conclusions from small samples are _____.
"In reality" signals a contrast with a _____ belief.

sentences.notes: "In reality"로 앞 문장의 "mistaken belief"와 명확한 대비를 만든다. "noisy"는 일상어이지만 통계적 맥락에서 "잡음이 많은(무작위 변동이 큰)"이라는 전문적 의미로 쓰인다. 콜론 뒤의 병렬 구조가 "noisy"의 의미를 구체화한다.

koreanAlignment.en: In reality
small samples are noisy
they fluctuate wildly from one draw to the next
koreanAlignment.ko: 한 번 추출할 때마다 크게 변동하며

koreanAlignment.en: and the conclusions they suggest are unstable

pronunciationNotes: fluctuate: /ˈflʌktʃueɪt/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "플럭추에이트"에 가깝다. -tuate를 빠르게 발음.
unstable: /ʌnˈsteɪbəl/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "언스테이블"에 가깝다. un- 접두사가 부정을 나타낸다.

sentences.original: This principle has practical consequences far beyond academic research.
sentences.translation: 이 원리는 학술 연구를 훨씬 넘어서는 실질적 결과를 가진다.

vocabulary.example: This theory has practical applications in daily life.
The consequences of small-sample thinking are everywhere.
The impact extends far beyond the laboratory.

expressions.usage: far가 beyond를 강조하여 적용 범위가 매우 넓다는 것을 나타낸다
이론적이 아닌 현실 세계에서의 영향을 가리키는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: This principle has [adjective] consequences far beyond [original domain]
patterns.explanation: 논의를 학술적 영역에서 실생활로 확장하는 전환 문장 패턴. "far beyond"가 적용 범위의 확대를 강조한다.
examples: This principle has economic consequences far beyond financial markets.
This insight has social consequences far beyond classroom settings.

drillQuestions.question: This principle has practical consequences far _____ academic research.
"Practical consequences" means effects in _____ life.
This sentence serves as a _____ to the next section of examples.
"Far beyond" means much _____ than a certain boundary.

sentences.notes: 일상 사례에서 더 넓은 실질적 영향으로의 전환을 수행하는 짧은 교량 문장. "far beyond academic research"가 이 원리의 적용 범위를 학술 영역 밖으로 확장시키며, 다음에 이어질 구체적 사례들을 예고한다.

koreanAlignment.en: This principle has practical consequences
far beyond academic research

pronunciationNotes: consequences: /ˈkɑːnsɪkwensɪz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "칸시퀀시즈"에 가깝다. que를 /kw/로 발음.
academic: /ˌækəˈdemɪk/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "애커데믹"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Product reviews based on five ratings are far less informative than those based on five hundred, yet consumers often treat them with equal seriousness.
sentences.translation: 다섯 개의 평점을 기반으로 한 제품 리뷰는 오백 개를 기반으로 한 것보다 훨씬 덜 유익하지만, 소비자들은 종종 그것들을 동등한 진지함으로 취급한다.

grammarElements.text: Product reviews based on five ratings
grammarElements.label: 주어 (과거분사 수식)

grammarElements.text: far less informative than those based on five hundred
grammarElements.label: 보어 (비교급 + 대명사 those)

grammarElements.text: yet consumers often treat them with equal seriousness
grammarElements.label: yet 양보절

vocabulary.example: Reviews with more data points are more informative.
Five-star ratings with few reviews can be misleading.
Consumers should weigh reviews with appropriate seriousness.

expressions.usage: far가 비교급 less를 강조하여 정보의 질 차이가 크다는 것을 나타낸다
차이가 있어야 할 것을 동일하게 취급하는 오류를 지적하는 표현

patterns.pattern: [Data source A] based on [small number] are far less [adjective] than those based on [large number], yet [people] often treat them with equal [noun]
patterns.explanation: 소량 데이터와 대량 데이터의 질적 차이를 비교급으로 명시한 뒤, yet으로 사람들이 그 차이를 무시하는 경향을 대비시키는 패턴.
examples: Survey results based on twenty responses are far less reliable than those based on two thousand, yet journalists often cite them with equal confidence.
Test scores based on three questions are far less meaningful than those based on thirty, yet parents often react to them with equal anxiety.

drillQuestions.question: Reviews based on five ratings are far less _____ than those based on five hundred.
Consumers often treat both with _____ seriousness.
"Those" in this sentence refers to _____.
"Yet" signals a _____ between what should happen and what does happen.

sentences.notes: 숫자 대비(five vs. five hundred)가 표본 크기의 차이를 직관적으로 보여준다. "those based on five hundred"에서 those가 앞의 "Product reviews"를 대신하는 대명사 용법이 중요하다. yet 뒤의 "equal seriousness"가 소비자의 인지적 오류를 날카롭게 지적한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Product reviews based on five ratings
koreanAlignment.ko: 다섯 개의 평점을 기반으로 한 제품 리뷰는

koreanAlignment.en: are far less informative than those based on five hundred
koreanAlignment.ko: 오백 개를 기반으로 한 것보다 훨씬 덜 유익하지만

koreanAlignment.en: yet consumers often treat them
with equal seriousness

pronunciationNotes: informative: /ɪnˈfɔːrmətɪv/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "인포머티브"에 가깝다. inform에서 파생.
seriousness: /ˈsɪəriəsnəs/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "시어리어스니스"에 가깝다. serious + -ness.

sentences.original: Medical case reports describing a handful of patients can generate dramatic headlines, but without larger trials, there is no reliable way to separate a genuine treatment effect from random variation or placebo response.
sentences.translation: 소수의 환자를 기술하는 의학 증례 보고는 극적인 헤드라인을 만들 수 있지만, 더 큰 규모의 임상시험 없이는 진짜 치료 효과를 무작위 변동이나 위약 반응으로부터 분리할 신뢰할 수 있는 방법이 없다.

grammarElements.text: Medical case reports describing a handful of patients
grammarElements.label: 주어 (현재분사 수식)

grammarElements.text: there is no reliable way to separate a genuine treatment effect from random variation or placebo response
grammarElements.label: 주절 (there is 존재구문 + to 부정사)

vocabulary.example: Case reports describe individual patient outcomes.
A handful of cases cannot prove a treatment works.
Larger trials are needed to confirm the effect.
The improvement may have been a placebo response.

expressions.usage: 매우 적은 수를 강조하는 비유적 표현
두 요인을 구별하는 것을 나타내는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: [Small-scale evidence] can generate [dramatic reaction], but without [larger-scale confirmation], there is no reliable way to separate [real effect] from [confounding factors]
patterns.explanation: 소규모 증거가 과도한 반응을 유발할 수 있음을 인정한 뒤, but으로 전환하여 대규모 확인 없이는 진짜 효과를 분리할 수 없다고 경고하는 패턴.
examples: A single successful experiment can generate enormous excitement, but without replication, there is no reliable way to separate a real discovery from experimental error.
Anecdotal success stories can generate powerful testimonials, but without controlled comparisons, there is no reliable way to separate genuine improvement from natural recovery.

drillQuestions.question: Medical case reports describe a _____ of patients.
Without larger trials, we cannot separate a genuine treatment effect from _____ variation.
A "placebo response" is improvement caused by _____ rather than actual treatment.
"Dramatic headlines" suggests the media _____ the significance of small studies.

sentences.notes: 의학 연구 맥락에서의 소표본 문제를 다룬다. "a handful of"가 일상적 표현으로 소규모를 강조하고, "separate A from B"가 통계적 분리의 개념을 전달한다. "placebo response"라는 전문 용어가 글의 학술적 깊이를 더한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Medical case reports describing a handful of patients
koreanAlignment.ko: 소수의 환자를 기술하는 의학 증례 보고는

koreanAlignment.en: can generate dramatic headlines
but without larger trials
there is no reliable way to separate
koreanAlignment.ko: 분리할 신뢰할 수 있는 방법이 없다

koreanAlignment.en: a genuine treatment effect from random variation or placebo response
koreanAlignment.ko: 진짜 치료 효과를 무작위 변동이나 위약 반응으로부터

pronunciationNotes: placebo: /pləˈsiːboʊ/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "플러시보"에 가깝다. 라틴어에서 유래.
genuine: /ˈdʒenjuɪn/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "제뉴인"에 가깝다. 마지막 e는 발음하지 않는다.

sentences.original: Even experienced professionals fall prey to small-sample thinking — doctors, investors, and coaches all tend to overweight patterns they have personally observed, forgetting that their experience is itself a small and potentially unrepresentative sample.
sentences.translation: 경험 많은 전문가들조차 소표본 사고의 먹잇감이 된다 — 의사, 투자자, 코치 모두 자신이 직접 관찰한 패턴에 과도한 비중을 두는 경향이 있으며, 자신의 경험 자체가 작고 잠재적으로 대표성이 없는 표본이라는 사실을 잊는다.

grammarElements.text: doctors, investors, and coaches all tend to overweight patterns they have personally observed
grammarElements.label: 대시 뒤 구체화 (열거 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: forgetting that their experience is itself a small and potentially unrepresentative sample
grammarElements.label: 분사구문 (이유 + that절)

vocabulary.example: Even experts fall prey to cognitive biases.
Investors tend to overweight recent performance.
Personal experience is often an unrepresentative sample.

expressions.usage: 지적이거나 심리적인 함정에 빠지는 것을 사냥 비유로 표현

expressions.phrase: their experience is itself a small ... sample
expressions.usage: itself가 "경험 자체도"라는 반전을 강조하는 핵심 구조

patterns.pattern: Even [respected group] fall prey to [error] — [list of professions] all tend to [biased behavior], forgetting that [their own data is limited]
patterns.explanation: "Even"으로 전문가도 예외가 아님을 강조한 뒤, 대시로 구체적 직업을 열거하고, 분사구문(forgetting that ~)으로 핵심 맹점을 지적하는 패턴.
examples: Even seasoned researchers fall prey to confirmation bias — physicists, biologists, and economists all tend to favor evidence that supports their theories, forgetting that their reading is itself a selective sample.

Even trained therapists fall prey to anchoring effects — counselors, psychiatrists, and social workers all tend to rely on initial impressions, forgetting that first sessions are themselves an incomplete picture.

drillQuestions.question: Even experienced professionals _____ prey to small-sample thinking.
Professionals tend to _____ patterns they have personally observed.
"Unrepresentative" means not _____ of the whole.
The sentence lists three professions: doctors, investors, and _____.

sentences.notes: "Even"으로 시작하여 전문가도 이 오류에서 자유롭지 않다는 점을 강조한다. "fall prey to"라는 사냥 은유가 인지적 취약성을 생동감 있게 전달하고, "their experience is itself a small ... sample"이 논증의 반전을 완성한다. 분사구문(forgetting that ~)이 맹점의 핵심을 간결하게 포착한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Even experienced professionals fall prey to small-sample thinking
koreanAlignment.ko: 경험 많은 전문가들조차 소표본 사고의 먹잇감이 된다

koreanAlignment.en: doctors, investors, and coaches
all tend to overweight patterns they have personally observed
koreanAlignment.ko: 모두 자신이 직접 관찰한 패턴에 과도한 비중을 두는 경향이 있으며

koreanAlignment.en: forgetting that their experience is itself
a small and potentially unrepresentative sample
koreanAlignment.ko: 작고 잠재적으로 대표성이 없는 표본이라는 사실을 잊는다

pronunciationNotes: prey: /preɪ/ — "프레이"에 가깝다. pray와 동음이의어. "먹잇감"이라는 뜻.
unrepresentative: /ˌʌnˌreprɪˈzentətɪv/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "언레프리젠터티브"에 가깝다. 여섯 음절.

sentences.original: The remedy is not to ignore small datasets entirely but to treat them as preliminary signals that require confirmation before they can guide important decisions.
sentences.translation: 해결책은 작은 데이터셋을 완전히 무시하는 것이 아니라, 중요한 결정을 안내하기 전에 확인이 필요한 예비 신호로 취급하는 것이다.

grammarElements.text: not to ignore small datasets entirely
grammarElements.label: 보어 1 (부정 to 부정사)

grammarElements.text: but to treat them as preliminary signals
grammarElements.label: 보어 2 (긍정 to 부정사)

grammarElements.text: that require confirmation before they can guide important decisions
grammarElements.label: 관계절 (before 시간절 내포)

vocabulary.example: The remedy for bad data is better methodology, not less data.
These are preliminary findings that need further testing.
The results require confirmation through larger studies.
Small datasets can still provide useful starting points.

expressions.usage: not A but B 구조로 올바른 접근법과 잘못된 접근법을 대비하는 격식적 표현
데이터의 잠정적 성격을 강조하는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: The remedy is not to [extreme response A] but to treat [subject] as [qualified category] that require [verification] before they can [influence outcome]
patterns.explanation: 극단적 반응을 부정한 뒤, 균형 잡힌 접근법을 "not A but B" 구조로 제시하는 처방적 패턴. 관계절이 조건을 명시한다.
examples: The remedy is not to dismiss intuition entirely but to treat it as a hypothesis that requires evidence before it can shape policy.
The remedy is not to avoid all risk but to treat initial results as indicators that require validation before they can justify major investments.

drillQuestions.question: The remedy is not to ignore small datasets but to treat them as _____ signals.
Preliminary signals require _____ before guiding decisions.
"The remedy" means the _____ to the problem.
"Not to ... but to ..." presents a _____ between wrong and right approaches.

sentences.notes: 글의 처방적 전환점. "not to ... but to ..." 구조가 극단적 반응(완전 무시)을 배제하고 균형 잡힌 접근(예비 신호로 취급)을 제시한다. "preliminary signals that require confirmation"이 데이터에 대한 건전한 태도를 핵심어로 요약한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The remedy is
not to ignore small datasets entirely
koreanAlignment.ko: 작은 데이터셋을 완전히 무시하는 것이 아니라

koreanAlignment.en: but to treat them as preliminary signals
that require confirmation
before they can guide important decisions

pronunciationNotes: preliminary: /prɪˈlɪmɪneri/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "프릴리미네리"에 가깝다. 다섯 음절.
confirmation: /ˌkɑːnfərˈmeɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "칸퍼메이션"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Asking "how large is the sample?" is one of the simplest and most powerful questions a critical thinker can learn to ask, and it applies equally to scientific papers, business reports, and the stories we tell ourselves about the world.
sentences.translation: "표본이 얼마나 큰가?"라고 묻는 것은 비판적 사고가가 배울 수 있는 가장 단순하면서도 가장 강력한 질문 중 하나이며, 과학 논문, 사업 보고서, 그리고 우리가 세상에 대해 스스로에게 하는 이야기에 동등하게 적용된다.

grammarElements.text: one of the simplest and most powerful questions a critical thinker can learn to ask
grammarElements.label: 보어 (최상급 2개 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: and it applies equally to scientific papers, business reports, and the stories we tell ourselves about the world
grammarElements.label: 병렬절 (3항 열거 + 관계절)

vocabulary.example: A critical thinker questions the evidence behind claims.
This principle applies to many different fields.
Scientific papers should report sample sizes clearly.

expressions.phrase: one of the simplest and most powerful
expressions.meaning: 가장 단순하면서도 가장 강력한 것 중 하나
expressions.usage: 두 최상급을 and로 병렬하여 단순성과 효과성을 동시에 강조하는 표현

expressions.phrase: the stories we tell ourselves about the world
expressions.meaning: 우리가 세상에 대해 스스로에게 하는 이야기
expressions.usage: 개인적 내러티브와 편향을 시적으로 표현. 자기 성찰적 톤을 부여한다

patterns.pattern: Asking "[practical question]" is one of the [superlative A] and [superlative B] questions [person] can learn to ask, and it applies equally to [domain 1], [domain 2], and [domain 3]
patterns.explanation: 글의 핵심 메시지를 하나의 실행 가능한 질문으로 압축한 뒤, 3항 열거로 적용 범위를 넓혀 보편적 가치를 부여하는 결론 패턴.
examples: Asking "what is the source?" is one of the simplest and most effective habits a reader can develop, and it applies equally to news articles, social media posts, and the opinions we absorb from friends.

Asking "what is the alternative?" is one of the most underused and most clarifying questions a decision-maker can ask, and it applies equally to career choices, medical options, and the assumptions we never examine.

drillQuestions.question: "How large is the sample?" is one of the simplest and most _____ questions.
A critical _____ can learn to ask this question.
This question applies equally to scientific papers, business reports, and _____.
"Applies equally" means it is _____ relevant to all three domains.

sentences.notes: 글의 결론으로, 전체 논증을 하나의 실행 가능한 질문("how large is the sample?")으로 압축한다. "the simplest and most powerful"의 이중 최상급이 이 질문의 가치를 강조하고, 마지막 열거에서 "the stories we tell ourselves about the world"라는 시적 표현이 개인적 편향까지 포괄하며 글을 성찰적으로 마무리한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Asking "how large is the sample?"
is one of the simplest and most powerful questions
koreanAlignment.ko: 가장 단순하면서도 가장 강력한 질문 중 하나이며

koreanAlignment.en: a critical thinker can learn to ask
and it applies equally to scientific papers, business reports
koreanAlignment.ko: 과학 논문, 사업 보고서에 동등하게 적용되고

koreanAlignment.en: and the stories we tell ourselves about the world
koreanAlignment.ko: 우리가 세상에 대해 스스로에게 하는 이야기에도

pronunciationNotes: sample: /ˈsæmpəl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "샘플"에 가깝다. 한국어에도 차용된 단어.
equally: /ˈiːkwəli/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "이퀄리"에 가깝다. equal + -ly.
